title: Editorial: It's time to step up and have your say for science
gist: An OMB rule proposed in late May would make political priorities the prime determinant of federal science funding, sideline peer review, and allow grant cancellations at any time—drawing united opposition from major scientific societies ahead of a July 13 public-comment deadline.

insights.claim: A late-May OMB proposed rule would make political priorities the prime determinant of federally funded science, sidelining peer review and giving political appointees final funding decisions.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: A politicized federal grant regime raises long-term uncertainty for US research institutions, universities, and biotech pipelines dependent on NIH funding.
insights.locator: Near the end of May, the Office of Management and Budget

insights.claim: The rule would allow the government to cancel any grant at any time after issuance and would base decisions on vague standards like 'in the national interest' and 'aligned with administration policies.'
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Mid-project cancellation risk makes multi-year research (clinical trials) unplannable, a structural threat to R&D-dependent sectors.
insights.locator: In Ars’ initial coverage of the OMB proposal

insights.claim: Unlike the executive orders it echoes, the OMB rule would carry the force of law and be significantly harder to challenge in court, making it more durable across administrations.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Codification into regulation raises the stakes—reversal would require rulemaking or legislation, not just a new executive order.
insights.quote: The proposed federal rule would establish regulations that would have politics shadow every research dollar, making it far harder to undo, no matter who holds office next.
insights.locator: The American Physical Society was equally blunt

insights.claim: The rule would require political appointees to approve any spending for conferences or publishing, injecting bureaucratic layers into basic scientific activities.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Administrative friction on publishing and travel would slow the pace and output of US-funded research.

insights.claim: Major bodies—AACR, American Cancer Society, American Heart Association, American Geophysical Union, American Physical Society, and AAAS—have all condemned the rule, describing it as the greatest threat US science funding has ever faced.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Breadth of opposition signals durable political controversy and litigation likelihood, prolonging funding uncertainty.
insights.quote: There is widespread agreement that this rule likely represents the greatest threat that US science funding has ever faced.

insights.claim: AACR criticized the administration's 'Gold Standard Science' concept as a term used to terminate research not because it is unsound but because it does not fit a preferred political agenda.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Reveals the rhetorical mechanism ('Gold Standard Science') by which politically disfavored research would be defunded.
insights.quote: a term the Administration uses to terminate research not because it is unsound but because it does not fit a preferred political or methodological agenda
insights.locator: The American Association for Cancer Research describes

insights.claim: Codifying shifting policy preferences would trigger repeated cycles of overhaul with each administration, creating a chronically unpredictable environment that makes multi-year clinical research hard to sustain.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Predictability loss directly harms clinical-trial-dependent drug development timelines.
insights.quote: Such back-and-forth would create a chronically unpredictable environment
insights.locator: Shane Jacobson, CEO of the American Cancer Society

insights.claim: The public-comment deadline is July 13, and high-quality individual comments create a legal record of opposition, force OMB to defend each provision, and signal to Congress that legislative intervention is warranted.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: The comment record shapes litigation ammunition and congressional appetite—key variables for whether the rule survives.
insights.locator: The sources we’ve consulted have consistently pointed out

insights.claim: Identical copy-pasted comments can be dismissed as a group, so only substantive, individualized comments force OMB to respond and preserve legal value.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Explains why mass-campaign tactics fail here; the effective lever is volume of distinct substantive comments.
insights.locator: Make sure your comment is high-quality

insights.claim: The author frames the rule as the biggest threat to US science since government-funded science took off after World War II, invoking Lysenkoism as the historical warning.
insights.speaker: John Timmer
insights.so_what: Historical framing (Lysenkoism) underscores the systemic risk of politically directed science to national competitiveness.
insights.quote: Lysenkoism is an example from history that makes that clear
insights.locator: All of us have benefited from the pursuit
